title: The Macro Minute: Will President Trump’s “Liberation Day” be more bark than bite?

Happy Monday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dell to
present our macro minute from Monday,
March 31st, 2025. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
leadoff morning note. If you would like
to review the analysis according to
these conclusions and to see if you need
to make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk,
you need to be a client of 42 macro. So
today's key macro question is, will
President Trump's liberation day be more
bark than bite? Our answers are the
tariff clock is ticking with US
reciprocal tariff deadline landing
Wednesday alongside Canada's planned
retalatory tariffs on $125 billion of US
goods. Fresh US tariffs of 25% on auto
imports take effect Thursday, setting
the stage for a historic trade reset
this week. Investors are hoping for
April 2nd to be a clearing event with
respect to US tariff policy, but we
expect uncertainty will linger. Clearly,
President Trump derives value from
keeping both investors and the US's
trading partners in the dark regarding
the final destination of evolving US
trade policy. If fully implemented, the
cumulative tariffs in discussion could
spike the US average tariff rate from
just under 2.5% at the start of
President Trump's second term to as high
as 35% which is a level unseen since the
late 19th century. The 28 percentage
point tariff shock from the current
rates that feature the recently
announced 20% tariffs on China among
other levies would deliver a 4% hit to
US GDP and push core PC inflation up by
2.5% over two to three years per the
Fed's models. A more tempered 14
percentage point tariff hike will still
cut GDP by 2% and boost core PC by 1.2%
over that same time frame.
April 2nd is shaping up as a pivotal
moment for a global trade and markets
where the difference between a
maximalist tariff regime and a
minimalist tariff regime could define
the trajectory for the Trump 2.0
economic agenda. This week will go a
long way to to determining if the Trump
administration is serious about
improving the lives of voters on Main
Street at Wall Street's transitory
expense. Markets are pricing against
central banking easing amid the policy
trade policy uncertainty and we continue
to view the Fed as behind the curve.
They should be doing QE now and if our
kitchen sinking thesis is proven
incrementally accurate, they will be
doing QE by year end likely after a
global debt refinancing air pocket.
Transitioning to our 42 micro dashboard.
As always, this one's uh we'll wrap up
with a question from our community. This
one's titled, can US equities continue
to outperform international? Says, as
gold begins to swap places with US
dollar, can US equities continue to
outperform international equities as
they have over the last several decades?
It seems like all the dollar denominated
assets are likely to suffer. I'm primary
wondering if KISS will be updated to
reflect this potential new regime change
although at this time it's too early to
tell. So I definitely agree that it's
much too early to tell but we continue
to highlight the the sort of underlying
risks associated with this question
which is the dam of money that has
flowed into US assets over the past few
decades and and really accelerated over
the past let's call it half decade or
so. If you look at the US's net
international investment deficit of
right around $24 trillion, that figure
more than doubled in the last five
years. So, you know, we've added
another, you know, let's call it
foreigners now own a net 13 trillion of
US assets uh sorry, their their net u
ownership of US assets grew by over 13
trillion uh to a net $24 trillion uh in
the past five years. That's a lot of
capital that can go uh in the other
direction uh in the coming you know
years and that's something that could
potentially cause a a significant uh
draw um decline in the US dollar a
secular decline in the US dollar uh much
like what we saw uh from let's call it
2002 all the way through let's call it
2007 where we saw down dollar
outperforming uh international and
emerging market markets relative to US
markets. Now the thing I'll say is that
just because the US international
markets are outperforming US markets
doesn't mean you need to swap your core
asset allocation to European equities or
emerging market equities. You know US
equities will go up over time. They just
may not go up as fast as international
equities or emerging market equities in
the context of a what could potentially
be a secular bare market uh in the US
dollar. But that doesn't mean you need
to change your core allocations. And
one, I would I would argue you probably
shouldn't change your core allocations
because at the end of the day, this
isn't going to be the only thing that's
driving returns. Don't forget, we have
things like AI and the potential
productivity boost that we could
potentially see. We have fiscal evolving
fiscal policy dynamics, involving trade
policy dynamics. Uh President Trump is,
you know, well in his way to rewriting
the the geopolitical world order. Uh and
so all these things could either wind up
being very favorable for US assets or
very unfavorable for US assets. And I
don't really know if we know um the
answer to that question yet. So uh I
would not be so much in a hurry uh to
make significant changes to your
strategic asset allocations in terms of
you know not being participating in the
US equity market and only participating
in the uh uh international equity market
or emerging market equities or European
equities. To me that seems like a very
you know kind of overreaction to
short-term price momentum. So uh we'll
wrap it up there. Jerry still here
presenting our macro minute for Monday,
March 31st, 2025. Best of luck out there
today. We'll catch you back here
tomorrow. Cheers.
